column21:
🧭 Gastric Motility

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer synthesis. Core supplies the standard adult solid-meal scintigraphic framework; RadPrimer supplies pediatric protocols, variable pediatric criteria, dual-phase behavior, milk studies, pitfalls, and all selected cases.

📖 Summary
  • Gastroparesis is delayed gastric emptying without mechanical obstruction; dumping is abnormally rapid gastric emptying.
  • Solid gastric emptying scintigraphy is the diagnostic test of choice for suspected gastroparesis.
  • Liquid and solid emptying are physiologically distinct and may be discordant; severe gastroparesis can coexist with normal liquid emptying.
  • Milk studies in infants can assess both reflux frequency/severity and gastric emptying, but pediatric normal values are sparse and depend on the consumed formula or liquid.

🔍 Nuclear Medicine
  • Core protocol: Tc-99m sulfur colloid or DTPA is mixed with an ingested meal; a standardized solid meal is imaged serially through 4 hours.
  • Core adult reference: normal residual gastric activity at 4 hours is <10%.
  • RadPrimer pediatric protocols commonly acquire images at time zero, 1 hour, 2 hours, and sometimes 4 hours with decay correction.
  • Young patients <8 years and <30 kg may be imaged with a single posterior camera; larger patients generally require dual-head imaging and geometric-mean calculation.
  • Low-energy collimation is used for solid studies; medium-energy collimation is used for dual-label liquid/solid studies.

📊 Pediatric Measurements
  • Pediatric delayed-emptying criteria are variable: published examples include retention >90% at 1 hour, >60% at 2 hours, or >30% at 4 hours; emptying ≤40% at 1 hour; or half-time >90 minutes for solids and >60 minutes for liquids.
  • Milk-study norms vary by technique and formula; a 1-hour value poorly predicts the 2-hour value, so imaging should continue for the full 2 hours.
  • For nonstandard meals or formulas without validated norms, percentage emptying or half-time may still aid clinical decisions, but interpretation must acknowledge the limitation.

⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Regions of interest are critical; poor placement reduces accuracy.
  • Prior upper-GI fluoroscopy, CT, or MRI can help define stomach shape and adjacent bowel for accurate ROI placement.
  • In rapid emptying, activity may leave the stomach before the time-zero image; calculation relative to total abdominal counts may be needed.
  • Geometric mean is generally the preferred calculation of retained activity because it reduces projection-dependent attenuation error.
  • Interpretation of abnormal 2-hour emptying with normal 4-hour emptying varies by institution.

🧬 Clinical / Path / Mechanism
  • Fundal accommodation and tonic fundal contraction support liquid emptying; antral peristalsis and trituration support solid emptying.
  • The pylorus restricts passage to particles approximately 1 mm or smaller.
  • Common pediatric causes include idiopathic, postviral, medication-related, and postsurgical etiologies.
  • Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, early satiety, bloating, abdominal pain, and weight loss; infants may show failure to thrive or irritability.

⭐ Super Summary
  • Use a standardized solid-meal scintigraphic study for gastroparesis, image long enough, separate liquid from solid physiology, and protect accuracy with correct ROI placement and geometric-mean processing.

  • Milk studies in infants can assess both reflux frequency/severity and gastric emptying, but pediatric normal values are sparse and depend on the consumed formula or liquid.

🔍 Nuclear Medicine
  • Core protocol: Tc-99m sulfur colloid or DTPA is mixed with an ingested meal; a standardized solid meal is imaged serially through 4 hours.
  • Core adult reference: normal residual gastric activity at 4 hours is <10%.
  • RadPrimer pediatric protocols commonly acquire images at time zero, 1 hour, 2 hours, and sometimes 4 hours with decay correction.

column11: Why can liquid and solid gastric emptying be discordant?
column12:
Liquid emptying depends mainly on fundal accommodation and tonic fundal contraction, whereas solid emptying requires antral peristalsis, trituration, and pyloric passage of particles approximately 1 mm or smaller.
Because these phases depend on different motor functions, one may be abnormal while the other remains normal; the scintigraphic curves must therefore be interpreted separately.

column11: Why is the geometric mean preferred for calculating retained gastric activity in larger patients?
column12: Anterior and posterior counts are affected differently by tissue attenuation as activity changes depth within the abdomen. Combining the two projections as a geometric mean reduces projection-dependent attenuation bias, producing a more reliable estimate of retained activity/emptying.

  • Solid gastric emptying scintigraphy is the diagnostic test of choice for suspected gastroparesis.
  • Liquid and solid emptying are physiologically distinct and may be discordant; severe gastroparesis can coexist with normal liquid emptying.
  • Milk studies in infants can assess both reflux frequency/severity and gastric emptying, but pediatric normal values are sparse and depend on the consumed formula or liquid.

🔍 Nuclear Medicine
  • Core protocol: Tc-99m sulfur colloid or DTPA is mixed with an ingested meal; a standardized solid meal is imaged serially through 4 hours.
  • Core adult reference: normal residual gastric activity at 4 hours is <10%.
  • RadPrimer pediatric protocols commonly acquire images at time zero, 1 hour, 2 hours, and sometimes 4 hours with decay correction.
  • Young patients <8 years and <30 kg may be imaged with a single posterior camera; larger patients generally require dual-head imaging and geometric-mean calculation.
  • Low-energy collimation is used for solid studies; medium-energy collimation is used for dual-label liquid/solid studies.

📊 Pediatric Measurements
  • Pediatric delayed-emptying criteria are variable: published examples include retention >90% at 1 hour, >60% at 2 hours, or >30% at 4 hours; emptying ≤40% at 1 hour; or half-time >90 minutes for solids and >60 minutes for liquids.
  • Milk-study norms vary by technique and formula; a 1-hour value poorly predicts the 2-hour value, so imaging should continue for the full 2 hours.
  • For nonstandard meals or formulas without validated norms, percentage emptying or half-time may still aid clinical decisions, but interpretation must acknowledge the limitation.

⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Regions of interest are critical; poor placement reduces accuracy.
  • Prior upper-GI fluoroscopy, CT, or MRI can help define stomach shape and adjacent bowel for accurate ROI placement.
  • In rapid emptying, activity may leave the stomach before the time-zero image; calculation relative to total abdominal counts may be needed.
  • Geometric mean is generally the preferred calculation of retained activity because it reduces projection-dependent attenuation error.
  • Interpretation of abnormal 2-hour emptying with normal 4-hour emptying varies by institution.

🧬 Clinical / Path / Mechanism
  • Fundal accommodation and tonic fundal contraction support liquid emptying; antral peristalsis and trituration support solid emptying.
  • The pylorus restricts passage to particles approximately 1 mm or smaller.
  • Common pediatric causes include idiopathic, postviral, medication-related, and postsurgical etiologies.
  • Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, early satiety, bloating, abdominal pain, and weight loss; infants may show failure to thrive or irritability.

⭐ Super Summary
  • Use a standardized solid-meal scintigraphic study for gastroparesis, image long enough, separate liquid from solid physiology, and protect accuracy with correct ROI placement and geometric-mean processing.

column11: Why can a time-zero image underestimate the true starting gastric activity in rapid emptying?
column12: If transit begins before the first acquired image, part of the meal has already entered bowel and is excluded from a stomach-only starting ROI. The apparent baseline gastric count is therefore too low. The source recommends considering total abdominal counts (stomach plus bowel) as the reference when this occurs.

column13: What is the pitfall of stopping an infant milk study after 1 hour, and what prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Assuming the 1-hour emptying value reliably predicts the 2-hour result.
Safety check: Continue imaging for the full 2 hours.
Discriminator: The source states that 1-hour emptying is a poor predictor of 2-hour emptying.
Why it happens: Early and later emptying rates are not interchangeable.

column13: What is the pitfall when the 2-hour value is abnormal but the 4-hour value is normal, and what prevents overcalling?
column14:
Trap: Labeling the study definitively abnormal from the 2-hour value alone.
Safety check: Review the completed 4-hour result and apply the institution's stated interpretive policy.
Discriminator: A normal 4-hour result may conflict with an abnormal 2-hour value.
Why it happens: Pediatric criteria and institutional interpretation are variable.

column13: What is the pitfall of using only one projection in a larger patient, and what prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Treating single-head counts as if attenuation were constant while activity changes depth.
Safety check: Use dual-head acquisition and geometric-mean calculation in larger patients.
Discriminator: The geometric mean is generally the preferred estimate of retained activity.
Why it happens: Anterior and posterior attenuation vary with gastric position and transit.

  • Young patients <8 years and <30 kg may be imaged with a single posterior camera; larger patients generally require dual-head imaging and geometric-mean calculation.
  • Low-energy collimation is used for solid studies; medium-energy collimation is used for dual-label liquid/solid studies.

📊 Pediatric Measurements
  • Pediatric delayed-emptying criteria are variable: published examples include retention >90% at 1 hour, >60% at 2 hours, or >30% at 4 hours; emptying ≤40% at 1 hour; or half-time >90 minutes for solids and >60 minutes for liquids.
  • Milk-study norms vary by technique and formula; a 1-hour value poorly predicts the 2-hour value, so imaging should continue for the full 2 hours.
  • For nonstandard meals or formulas without validated norms, percentage emptying or half-time may still aid clinical decisions, but interpretation must acknowledge the limitation.

⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Regions of interest are critical; poor placement reduces accuracy.
  • Prior upper-GI fluoroscopy, CT, or MRI can help define stomach shape and adjacent bowel for accurate ROI placement.
  • In rapid emptying, activity may leave the stomach before the time-zero image; calculation relative to total abdominal counts may be needed.
  • Geometric mean is generally the preferred calculation of retained activity because it reduces projection-dependent attenuation error.
  • Interpretation of abnormal 2-hour emptying with normal 4-hour emptying varies by institution.

🧬 Clinical / Path / Mechanism
  • Fundal accommodation and tonic fundal contraction support liquid emptying; antral peristalsis and trituration support solid emptying.
  • The pylorus restricts passage to particles approximately 1 mm or smaller.
  • Common pediatric causes include idiopathic, postviral, medication-related, and postsurgical etiologies.
  • Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, early satiety, bloating, abdominal pain, and weight loss; infants may show failure to thrive or irritability.

⭐ Super Summary
  • Use a standardized solid-meal scintigraphic study for gastroparesis, image long enough, separate liquid from solid physiology, and protect accuracy with correct ROI placement and geometric-mean processing.

column15: What milk-study normal ranges are listed for infant gastric emptying?
column16:
Signer, 1975: 48–70% emptying at 60 min and 24–48% emptying at 2 hours.
Knatten et al., 2013: 43–79% emptying at 60 min.
Exam pivot: Norms depend on the specific consumed liquid or formula.

column15: What can a milk scintigraphy study assess in infants?
column16:
Frequency and proximal severity of gastroesophageal reflux plus gastric emptying.
Milk and formula behave more like solids than liquids in the acidic stomach.
